items.front: When must controllers become familiar with pertinent weather information?
items.back: When coming on duty.

items.front: What weather awareness must continue after a controller comes on duty?
items.back: Stay aware of current and forecast weather information needed to perform ATC duties.

items.front: Why does 2-6-1 require continuing weather awareness?
items.back: Because weather information is needed to perform ATC duties safely and effectively.

items.front: What does the note under 2-6-1 say about weather and phases of flight?
items.back: Every phase of flight has the potential to be impacted by weather.

items.front: Which two PIREP categories receive special emphasis in 2-6-2?
items.back: Urgent (UUA) and routine (UA) PIREPs.

items.front: What ceiling threshold triggers PIREP solicitation under 2-6-2?
items.back: Ceilings at or below 5,000 feet.

items.front: What hourly PIREP requirement applies when approach control services are provided with ceilings at or below 5,000 feet?
items.back: Obtain at least one descent or climb-out PIREP each hour, plus related weather phenomena when available.

items.front: How is a low-level wind shear PIREP classified when the pilot reports airspeed fluctuations of 10 knots or more?
items.back: Urgent.

items.front: How is a low-level wind shear PIREP classified when reported airspeed fluctuations are less than 10 knots?
items.back: Routine.

items.front: How is a sulfur-gas-only cabin report classified when the pilot confirms no volcanic ash clouds are present?
items.back: Routine.

items.front: What extra details must be recorded with a turbulence PIREP?
items.back: Duration and intensity.

items.front: What extra details must be recorded with an icing PIREP?
items.back: Icing type and intensity, plus the air temperature in which the icing is occurring.

items.front: How are operationally significant PIREPs disseminated by en route facilities?
items.back: Relay them to the facility weather coordinator.

items.front: When prevailing visibility is less than 4 miles, which value is used for aircraft operations if tower and surface observations differ?
items.back: Use the lower of the two observations.

items.front: When must tower visibility observations be forwarded to the weather observer?
items.back: When prevailing visibility is less than 4 miles and tower observations are being taken.

items.front: At what wind speed is the wind described as calm?
items.back: Less than three knots.

items.front: Which ceiling and visibility changes must be forwarded to the appropriate control facility?
items.back: Drops below the listed thresholds and improvements back above them.

items.front: What special weather reporting requirement applies at airports where military turbo-jet en route descents are routinely conducted?
items.back: Report the conditions to the ARTCC even if it is not the controlling facility.

items.front: What happens if the receiving facility says weather reports are not required for a specific time period?
items.back: Discontinue the reports for that period.

items.front: How may the area of weather or chaff coverage be defined when issuing information to aircraft?
items.back: By azimuth and distance from the aircraft, and/or by the width and coverage of the area in terms of fixes or distance and direction from fixes.

items.front: What term is used for satellite-derived weather?
items.back: Weather.

items.front: What term is used for radar-derived weather?
items.back: Precipitation.

items.front: What word should not be used when describing radar-derived weather?
items.back: Turbulence.

items.front: How do STARS precipitation levels correlate to intensity?
items.back: Level 1 light, Level 2 moderate, Levels 3 and 4 heavy, Levels 5 and 6 extreme.

items.front: How do en route controllers describe the lowest and highest displayable precipitation intensities?
items.back: The lowest displayable intensity is moderate, and the highest is heavy to extreme.

items.front: What must be issued when approving a weather deviation for an aircraft that had previously been issued a crossing altitude, including climb via or descend via?
items.back: Assign an altitude to maintain and, if necessary, a speed, and advise if the aircraft is expected to resume the procedure.

items.front: What does /F mean in en route fourth-line weather-deviation coordination?
items.back: The pilot has been authorized to deviate and rejoin the route of flight at the next NAVAID or waypoint in the flight plan.

items.front: What does the absence of a NAVAID, waypoint, or /F in the fourth line indicate?
items.back: It means the aircraft was approved to deviate for weather only; the receiving controller must clear it back on route, or verbal coordination is required if fourth-line text is insufficient.

items.front: What should the transferring controller coordinate when forwarding weather deviation information?
items.back: The nature of the route guidance service, including assigned headings, suggested headings, or pilot-initiated deviation.

items.front: What kind of weather information may be transmitted without consulting the weather reporting station?
items.back: General weather information without specific values, and elements derived directly from instruments, pilots, or radar.

items.front: What are the approved sources for transmitting specific ceiling and visibility values?
items.back: Use the official observer, a weather-station report, an FAA-approved automation surface weather system, or a properly certificated controller acting as the official observer.

items.front: May USAF controllers serve as official weather observers?
items.back: No.

items.front: What must be done when tower-observed weather elements differ from the weather station report?
items.back: Report the difference to the official observer for the element concerned.

items.front: Within what distance of the sector or area of jurisdiction must pilots be advised of hazardous weather that may impact operations?
items.back: Within 150 NM.

items.front: Which hazardous weather products are explicitly included in 2-6-6 advisories?
items.back: AIRMET outside the CONUS, SIGMET, Convective SIGMET, urgent PIREP, and Center Weather Advisory.

items.front: How must controllers disseminate hazardous inflight weather information on frequencies?
items.back: Broadcast it on all frequencies except the emergency frequency.

items.front: Where should pilots be sent if they request additional hazardous weather information?
items.back: The nearest Flight Service.

items.front: When may a tower cab or approach control facility limit hazardous weather broadcasts to events within 50 NM?
items.back: When any part of the described area is within 50 NM of the airspace under the facility's jurisdiction.

items.front: What must en route controllers do with hazardous weather information messages in ERAM after appropriate action has been taken?
items.back: Electronically acknowledge them.
